learning_objectives: Classify cybercrime by type, motivation, and impact
Understand federal and state cybercrime statutes
Apply legal standards for digital evidence in criminal cases
readings: Cybercrime: Criminal Threats from Cyberspace Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Cybercrime investigation combines digital forensics with traditional investigative methodology. Understanding the legal framework is essential because improperly collected evidence is inadmissible.
key_concepts: Cybercrime categories: financial, espionage, hacktivism, terrorism, exploitation
Legal framework: CFAA, Wire Fraud (18 USC 1343), Identity Theft (18 USC 1028A)
Digital evidence standards: Federal Rules of Evidence, authentication, best evidence rule
Jurisdiction challenges: where did the crime occur in cyberspace?
Law enforcement cooperation: FBI, Secret Service, HSI roles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a real cybercrime case from indictment to conviction, mapping the evidence chain and legal arguments

review.value: Digital evidence must be authenticated under FRE 901(b)(4): appearance, contents, internal patterns, or other distinctive characteristics. Hash values and chain of custody documentation establish authenticity.
Federal jurisdiction if: crime crosses state lines, involves federal computer systems, involves interstate wire communications, or exceeds $5,000 in damage. State jurisdiction for local crimes.

learning_objectives: Collect digital evidence following forensically sound procedures
Obtain and execute search warrants for digital evidence
Preserve evidence from volatile and cloud sources
readings: DOJ Searching and Seizing Computers manual

lecture_notes.introduction: Evidence collection in cybercrime investigations must satisfy both forensic integrity requirements and legal standards. A technically perfect analysis is useless if the evidence was improperly obtained.
key_concepts: Search warrant requirements: probable cause, particularity, scope limitations
Consent searches and exceptions to warrant requirements
Forensic imaging: legal requirements for bit-for-bit copies
Cloud evidence: legal process for service provider data (SCA, MLAT)
Preservation orders: preventing evidence destruction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drafting a search warrant affidavit for a cybercrime case, specifying what to seize and why, with technical justification

review.value: The Fourth Amendment requires warrants to particularly describe the place to be searched and items to be seized. For digital evidence: specify devices, data types, date ranges, and search methodology.
18 USC 2703: law enforcement can compel service providers to disclose stored communications. Subpoena for basic subscriber info, court order for transactional records, warrant for content.

learning_objectives: Investigate Business Email Compromise and wire fraud schemes
Trace cryptocurrency transactions on the blockchain
Follow the money: financial forensics in cybercrime

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial cybercrime causes billions in annual losses. Investigating these crimes requires understanding both the technical attack vectors and the financial systems used to launder proceeds.
key_concepts: BEC investigation: email header analysis, domain analysis, money flow tracing
Cryptocurrency tracing: blockchain explorers, clustering analysis
Money mule networks and laundering techniques
Bank cooperation: SAR (Suspicious Activity Reports), asset freezing
International cooperation for cross-border financial crime
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tracing a Bitcoin transaction from a ransomware payment through mixing services using blockchain analysis tools

review.value: Bitcoin transactions are pseudonymous, not anonymous. Chain analysis companies (Chainalysis, Elliptic) cluster addresses to entities. Mixing services and privacy coins (Monero) complicate but do not eliminate tracing.
Examine email headers for true origin (Received: headers), analyze lookalike domains (WHOIS, registration date), trace wire transfer recipients, and coordinate with receiving banks for fund recovery.

learning_objectives: Investigate network intrusions using forensic evidence
Trace attackers through proxy chains and VPNs
Build criminal cases from network forensic evidence
readings: Network Forensics: Tracking Hackers through Cyberspace Ch. 8-12

lecture_notes.introduction: Network intrusion investigations trace the attacker's path from their infrastructure through the victim's network. Building a prosecutable case requires linking digital evidence to a specific individual.
key_concepts: Attack infrastructure analysis: C2 servers, bulletproof hosting, domain registration
WHOIS analysis and hosting provider cooperation
IP attribution challenges: VPNs, Tor, proxy chains
Log evidence: firewall, DNS, DHCP, authentication logs
Building the attribution chain: digital evidence to physical identity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Investigating a simulated network intrusion: tracing the attacker from IDS alerts back through proxy infrastructure to identify the likely source

review.value: IDS alert -> source IP -> hosting provider records (via subpoena) -> payment information -> identity. Each link requires legal process and may involve multiple jurisdictions.

Tor anonymizes source IP through three relays. Investigation techniques: traffic correlation (timing analysis), operational security failures by the suspect, controlled server exploitation, and informants.

learning_objectives: Navigate the dark web safely for investigative purposes
Identify and de-anonymize dark web actors through OPSEC failures
Understand legal authorities for dark web investigations

lecture_notes.introduction: The dark web hosts criminal marketplaces, forums, and services. Investigating these spaces requires specialized techniques and strict operational security.
key_concepts: Tor network architecture and anonymity properties
Dark web marketplace investigation techniques
De-anonymization: OPSEC failures, cryptocurrency tracing, undercover operations
Legal authorities: Network Investigative Techniques (court-authorized hacking)
Evidence preservation from ephemeral dark web content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the Silk Road investigation to identify the OPSEC failures that led to Ross Ulbricht's identification and arrest

review.value: Most dark web arrests result from OPSEC failures: reusing usernames across clear and dark web, cryptocurrency linked to real identity, geolocation metadata in photos, and social engineering by undercover agents.

Court-authorized technique where FBI deploys code on a dark web server to identify connecting users by collecting their real IP addresses. Used in Playpen case. Controversial: one warrant authorized searching thousands of computers.

learning_objectives: Write expert forensic reports for legal proceedings
Understand the role of expert witnesses in cybercrime trials

lecture_notes.introduction: The ultimate goal of a cybercrime investigation is successful prosecution. This requires presenting complex technical evidence in a way that judges and juries can understand.
key_concepts: Daubert standard: scientific validity of expert testimony
Expert report writing: methodology, findings, opinions, basis for opinions
Presenting technical evidence to non-technical audiences
Cross-examination preparation: defending methodology and conclusions
Chain of custody testimony
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock trial exercise: presenting digital forensic evidence, defending analysis methodology under cross-examination

review.value: Judge evaluates whether expert testimony is based on: (1) testable methodology, (2) peer-reviewed techniques, (3) known error rate, (4) generally accepted methods. Digital forensic tools must meet these criteria.
Use analogies: 'IP addresses are like phone numbers, they identify the connection, not necessarily the person.' Avoid jargon. Use visual aids. Focus on what the evidence means, not how you found it.

quizzes.topic: Legal Framework, Evidence Collection, and Financial Crime

questions.question: During a cybercrime investigation, you discover that the suspect uses Gmail for C2 communication. Describe the legal process required to obtain the email contents from Google, including the specific statute, the legal standard, and what information you can obtain at each legal threshold.
questions.answer: Under the Stored Communications Act (18 USC 2703): (1) Subpoena (2703(c)): basic subscriber information (name, address, IP logs, payment info). No judicial approval needed, just a grand jury or administrative subpoena. (2) Court order under 2703(d): transactional records (email headers, to/from addresses, timestamps, but not content). Requires 'specific and articulable facts' showing relevance to the investigation. (3) Search warrant (2703(a)): full email content. Requires probable cause reviewed by a judge. For emails older than 180 days, 2703(b) historically allowed a subpoena for content, but post-Carpenter, most courts require a warrant for all email content. Google requires a warrant for content regardless of age. Process: draft a search warrant affidavit establishing probable cause, submit to a federal magistrate, serve the warrant on Google's Law Enforcement Response Team (LERT) via their legal process system.

rubric.key: Accurate information obtainable at each level

questions.question: A ransomware victim paid 5 Bitcoin to the attacker's wallet address. Describe the investigative steps to trace this payment, the challenges you would encounter, and what legal process would be needed to identify the attacker.
questions.answer: Tracing steps: (1) Record the payment transaction ID and destination wallet address from the victim's Bitcoin client. (2) Use blockchain analysis tools (Chainalysis, blockchain.com explorer) to track where the 5 BTC moves from the destination address. (3) Identify if the funds move to a known exchange, mixing service, or convert to privacy coins. (4) If funds reach a regulated exchange (Coinbase, Kraken): serve a subpoena/warrant on the exchange for the account holder's identity (KYC records: name, address, ID document, bank account). Challenges: (1) Mixing/tumbling services break the transaction chain by pooling and redistributing funds. (2) Chain-hopping: converting BTC to Monero (privacy coin) which is untraceable. (3) Decentralized exchanges have no KYC. (4) Jurisdiction: the exchange may be offshore. Legal process: grand jury subpoena for exchange account records (domestic), MLAT for international exchanges, or cooperation through Interpol/Europol.

quizzes.topic: Network Investigation, Dark Web, and Prosecution

questions.question: An IDS alerts on a connection from your network to a known C2 server at IP 203.0.113.50. Describe the investigative steps to build a criminal case, from the initial alert through to identifying the responsible individual, including the legal process at each step.
questions.answer: Step 1 (internal): Identify the internal host connecting to the C2 IP from firewall/proxy logs. Image the host's memory and disk. Analyze for malware to confirm compromise (this is the victim host, not necessarily the suspect). Step 2 (C2 server): Issue a preservation letter to the hosting provider for IP 203.0.113.50 to prevent log deletion. Obtain a court order/warrant for hosting provider records: subscriber information, payment records, server logs. Step 3 (follow the registration): The hosting provider reveals the account was registered with a prepaid card purchased at a specific store. Subpoena the store for surveillance footage at the time of purchase. Step 4 (expand investigation): Analyze the C2 server logs (obtained via warrant) for other victim connections and the attacker's administrative access source IP. Trace administrative access through similar legal process. Step 5 (identification): Correlate all evidence: payment records, IP addresses, online identifiers, linguistic patterns in C2 communications. Present to AUSA for prosecution decision.

rubric.key: Correct investigative sequence from alert to identification
Appropriate legal process at each step
Evidence preservation and chain of custody

questions.question: You are called as an expert witness to testify about digital forensic evidence in a cybercrime trial. The defense attorney challenges the reliability of your forensic imaging process, arguing that the hash values could have been altered. How would you defend the integrity of your evidence under the Daubert standard?
questions.answer: Daubert defense: (1) Testable methodology: Forensic imaging with dc3dd produces SHA-256 hashes of both the source drive and the resulting image simultaneously. The same imaging process can be repeated independently to produce the same hash, verifying the methodology is testable and reproducible. (2) Peer review: The forensic imaging process follows NIST SP 800-86 and SWGDE best practices, which are peer-reviewed published standards used by law enforcement agencies worldwide. (3) Known error rate: SHA-256 has a collision probability of 1 in 2^128 (effectively zero). The hash verification process has a known and negligibly small error rate. (4) General acceptance: Forensic imaging with hash verification is standard practice accepted by every forensic laboratory and law enforcement agency. It has been upheld in hundreds of court proceedings. Additional: Testify that the write-blocker prevented any modification to the source, the hash was calculated at time of imaging AND verified independently before analysis, and the evidence was stored in a locked evidence room with documented chain of custody.

rubric.key: Technically accurate hash and forensic process explanation
Chain of custody and write-blocking defense

major_assignments.title: Cybercrime Investigation Case Study
major_assignments.description: Investigate a simulated cybercrime scenario from initial report through evidence collection, analysis, and case preparation. Produce a complete investigative file suitable for prosecution.
objectives: Collect and preserve digital evidence following legal standards
Trace the attacker through digital and financial evidence
deliverables: Evidence collection logs with chain of custody
Expert forensic report suitable for court submission

criteria: All evidence sources identified and pursued
Clear narrative connecting evidence to suspect
Forensically sound evidence with maintained chain of custody
Legally correct documentation suitable for prosecution
Professional case file ready for prosecution review

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of cybercrime law and investigation techniques

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on evidence collection, analysis, and case preparation exercises

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering legal framework, evidence, and financial crime

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all cybercrime investigation topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions and mock trial exercises. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Investigation discussions are encouraged. All case files and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Investigation exercises use simulated scenarios only. Conducting unauthorized investigations of real individuals is illegal and grounds for expulsion.
course_policies.professional_conduct: Case study materials may contain sensitive topics (exploitation, fraud). Maintain professional conduct and respect for victims throughout the course.
